The astropy feedstock has been split into two packages, astropy-base only has required dependencies and astropy now has all optional dependencies. To maintain the old behavior you should migrate to astropy-base. Thus I made this PR. Notes and instructions for merging this PR: 1. Make sure that the recipe can indeed only depend on `astropy-base`. 2. Please merge the PR only after the tests have passed. 3. Feel free to push to the bot's branch to update this PR if needed. If this PR was opened in error or needs to be updated please add the `bot-rerun` label to this PR. The bot will close this PR and schedule another one. If you do not have permissions to add this label, you can use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerun bot in a PR comment to have the `conda-forge-admin` add it for you. This PR was created by the [regro-cf-autotick-bot](https://github.com/regro/cf-scripts). According to https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/figure/figure_intro.html#saving-figures:

> Many types of output are supported, including raster formats like

However, GIF support was broken by #6178. Restore GIF support.

Description

vstack fails for tables of zero rows that have SkyCoord columns.

### Expected behavior

The code below should succeed, and `table2` should be a new table with zero rows.

### How to Reproduce

```pycon
>>> from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
>>> from astropy.table import Table, vstack
>>> table1 = Table({'foo': SkyCoord([], [], unit='deg')})
>>> table2 = vstack((table1, table1))

Description

Table aggregation by sum does not correctly handle zero-length arrays. Instead, it prints a warning and drops the numerical columns.

Zero-length arrays are not a problem for Numpy...

```pycon
>>> np.sum([])
np.float64(0.0)
>>> np.add.reduce([])
np.float64(0.0)
```

### Expected behavior

The code sample below should produce the following output (and no warnings):

```
<Table length=0>
action duration
 str1  float64 
------ --------
```

### How to Reproduce

```pycon
>>> from astropy.table import Table
>>> import numpy as np
>>> table = Table({
...     "action": np.asarray([], dtype=str),
...     "duration": np.asarray([], dtype=float)
... })
>>> table.group_by("action").groups.aggregate(np.sum)
WARNING: Cannot aggregate column 'duration' with type 'float64': index 0 out-of-bounds in add.reduceat [0, 0) [astropy.table.groups]
<Table length=0>
action
 str1 
------
```

### Versions

```
macOS-14.7.1-arm64-arm-64bit
Python 3.11.10 (main, Sep  7 2024, 05:47:53) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
astropy 6.1.5
Numpy 2.1.3
pyerfa 2.0.1.4
Scipy 1.14.1
Matplotlib 3.9.2
```

Release notes

v5.0.2

What's Changed

Full Changelog: https://github.com/codecov/codecov-action/compare/v5.0.1...v5.0.2

v5.0.1

What's Changed

  • fix: use marketplace v5 badge by @​thomasrockhu-codecov in codecov/codecov-action#1646
  • fix: update tokenless branch logic by @​thomasrockhu-codecov in codecov/codecov-action#1650
  • chore(release): 5.0.1 by @​thomasrockhu-codecov in codecov/codecov-action#1656

Full Changelog: https://github.com/codecov/codecov-action/compare/v5.0.0...v5.0.1

v5.0.0

v5 Release

v5 of the Codecov GitHub Action will use the Codecov Wrapper to encapsulate the CLI. This will help ensure that the Action gets updates quicker.

Migration Guide

The v5 release also coincides with the opt-out feature for tokens for public repositories. In the Global Upload Token section of the settings page of an organization in codecov.io, you can set the ability for Codecov to receive a coverage reports from any source. This will allow contributors or other members of a repository to upload without needing access to the Codecov token. For more details see how to upload without a token.

[!WARNING]
The following arguments have been changed

  • file (this has been deprecated in favor of files)
  • plugin (this has been deprecated in favor of plugins)

The following arguments have been added:

  • binary
  • gcov_args
  • gcov_executable
  • gcov_ignore
  • gcov_include
  • report_type
  • skip_validation
  • swift_project

You can see their usage in the action.yml file.

What's Changed

  • chore(deps): bump to eslint9+ and remove eslint-config-google by @​thomasrockhu-codecov in codecov/codecov-action#1591

  • build(deps): bump @​actions/core from 1.11.0 to 1.11.1 by @​dependabot in codecov/codecov-action#1597
  • build(deps): bump github/codeql-action from 3.26.9 to 3.26.11 by @​dependabot in codecov/codecov-action#1596
  • build(deps-dev): bump @​typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin from 8.8.0 to 8.8.1 by @​dependabot in codecov/codecov-action#1600
  • build(deps-dev): bump eslint from 9.11.1 to 9.12.0 by @​dependabot in codecov/codecov-action#1598

Description

Implemented a default bounding box for compound models. The bounding box is deduced from the compound model's operands. For example, the bounding box of the sum of two models is the union of the bounding boxes of the individual models.

Fixes #16951.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2420582764","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16953#issuecomment-2420582764","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953","id":2420582764,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAB_CCc6QRyls","user":{"login":"lpsinger","id":728407,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyODQwNw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/728407?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger","html_url":"https://github.com/lpsinger","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-17T21:12:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-17T21:12:03Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> The bounding box is supposed to define the interval over which the model is defined...

No, according to [the documentation](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/modeling/models.html#bounding-boxes):

> All [Model](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.modeling.Model.html#astropy.modeling.Model) subclasses have a [bounding_box](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.modeling.Model.html#astropy.modeling.Model.bounding_box) attribute that can be used to set the limits over which the model is significant. This greatly improves the efficiency of evaluation when the input range is much larger than the characteristic width of the model itself.

I take it that "significant" means significant in magnitude, i.e., nonzero. So the bounding box describes the interval over which the function has support. 

> I fail see why `+` and `-` bounding boxes should be unions, while `*` is an intersection.

With the above clarification, do you see why?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2420582764/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/pulls/comments/1805441850","pull_request_review_id":2376311900,"id":1805441850,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAB_CCc5rnNs6","diff_hunk":"@@ -3319,6 +3318,53 @@ def evaluate(self, *args, **kw):
 
         return self._apply_operators_to_value_lists(leftval, rightval, **kw)
 
+    def _bounding_box(self):
+        if self.op == "fix_inputs":
+            raise NotImplementedError
+
+        left = self.left.get_bounding_box()
+        right = self.right.get_bounding_box()
+
+        match self.op, left, right:
+            case _, CompoundBoundingBox(), CompoundBoundingBox():
+                raise NotImplementedError(
+                    "No bounding box is defined for this model. A default compound bounding box for a compound model is not yet implemented."
+                )
+            case "+" | "-", ModelBoundingBox(), ModelBoundingBox():
+                return [
+                    (min(l.lower, r.lower), max(l.upper, r.upper))
+                    for l, r in zip(
+                        left.intervals.values(),
+                        right.intervals.values(),
+                    )
+                ]
+            case "*", ModelBoundingBox(), None:
+                return left
+            case "*", None, ModelBoundingBox():
+                return right
+            case "*", ModelBoundingBox(), ModelBoundingBox():
+                result = [
+                    (max(l.lower, r.lower), min(l.upper, r.upper))
+                    for l, r in zip(left.intervals.values(), right.intervals.values())

Fixes #16951.

Description

Implemented a default bounding box for compound models. The bounding box is deduced from the compound model's operands. For example, the bounding box of the sum of two models is the union of the bounding boxes of the individual models.

Fixes #16951.

:"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nstarman/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"open_issues":50,"closed_issues":407,"state":"open","created_at":"2023-10-14T22:16:29Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T08:52:36Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":13,"created_at":"2024-09-05T21:31:40Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T13:12:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/pulls/16953","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16953","diff_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16953.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16953.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"### Description

Implemented a default bounding box for compound models. The bounding box is deduced from the compound model's operands. For example, the bounding box of the sum of two models is the union of the bounding boxes of the individual models.

Fixes #16951.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2422447777","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/16953#issuecomment-2422447777","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16953","id":2422447777,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAB_CCc6QY56h","user":{"login":"lpsinger","id":728407,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyODQwNw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/728407?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger","html_url":"https://github.com/lpsinger","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-18T13:12:02Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T13:12:02Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> This choice of wording in the docs is perhaps misleading, as it doesn't represent the full use cases of bounding boxes in astropy models. A simple example is a `Polynomial2D` model that maps distortion in a focal plane of a telescope, and if the focal plane distortion is symmetric about the center, then the model's "significance" is where it has been measured, i.e. in the focal plane, and not outside it. The choice of input bounding box is independent whether the model evaluates to zero, and in fact, in the symmetric case, it will evaluate to zero at the center, and to negative values. And all this has nothing to do with something intrinsic about the functional form, but rather the valid domain over which the model was fit.

Hmm. I didn't realize that the interpretation of bounding box was not consistent across models.

There are several distinct sets that we may need or want to model for automated evaluation and integration of compound models:

1. support (model is defined and of significant magnitude, i.e., real and nonzero)
2. domain (where the model has valid values; model should return NaN or raise an error if evaluated outside of this set)
3. values are known (model may be formally defined outside of this set, but it is not to be believed: it may be an extrapolation or it may just be garbage)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/2422447777/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

v4.2.2

  • Expand unit test coverage for isGhes by @​jww3 in actions/checkout#1946

v4.2.1

  • Check out other refs/* by commit if provided, fall back to ref by @​orhantoy in actions/checkout#1924

v4.2.0

  • Add Ref and Commit outputs by @​lucacome in actions/checkout#1180
  • Dependency updates by @​dependabot- actions/checkout#1777, actions/checkout#1872

  • Bump the minor-npm-dependencies group across 1 directory with 4 updates by @​dependabot in actions/checkout#1739

  • Pin actions/checkout's own workflows to a known, good, stable version. by @​jww3 in actions/checkout#1776

v4.1.6

  • Check platform to set archive extension appropriately by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1732

v4.1.5

  • Update NPM dependencies by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1703
  • Bump github/codeql-action from 2 to 3 by @​dependabot in actions/checkout#1694

  • README: Suggest user.email to be 41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1707

v4.1.4

  • Disable extensions.worktreeConfig when disabling sparse-checkout by @​jww3 in actions/checkout#1692
  • Add dependabot config by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1688
  • Bump the minor-actions-dependencies group with 2 updates by @​dependabot in actions/checkout#1693
  • Bump word-wrap from 1.2.3 to 1.2.5 by @​dependabot in actions/checkout#1643

v4.1.3

  • Check git version before attempting to disable sparse-checkout by @​jww3 in actions/checkout#1656
  • Add SSH user parameter by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1685
  • Update actions/checkout version in update-main-version.yml by @​jww3 in actions/checkout#1650

v4.1.2

  • Fix: Disable sparse checkout whenever sparse-checkout option is not present @​dscho in actions/checkout#1598

v4.1.1

  • Correct link to GitHub Docs by @​peterbe in actions/checkout#1511
  • Link to release page from what's new section by @​cory-miller in actions/checkout#1514

v4.1.0

  • Add support for partial checkout filters

If this PR was opened in error or needs to be updated please add the `bot-rerun` label to this PR. The bot will close this PR and schedule another one. If you do not have permissions to add this label, you can use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerun bot in a PR comment to have the `conda-forge-admin` add it for you. Request","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-05T18:05:55Z","updated_at":"2024-09-05T18:05:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### What is the problem this feature will solve?

There is a common need to be able to integrate compound models (e.g., synphot). Much of the complex machinery to enable this relies on guessing the limits of integration.

Many models self-document the domain over which they have significant values using the `bounding_box` property. The exception is compound models, which don't provide a useful default bounding box implementation.

### Describe the desired outcome

Implement a default bounding box method for compound models that determines the bounding box by traversing the model tree (recursively) and combining intervals appropriately.

### Additional context

According to the [documentation](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/modeling/models.html#efficient-model-rendering-with-bounding-boxes):

> All [Model](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.modeling.Model.html#astropy.modeling.Model) subclasses have a [bounding_box](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.modeling.Model.html#astropy.modeling.Model.bounding_box) attribute that can be used to set the limits over which the model is significant. This greatly improves the efficiency of evaluation when the input range is much larger than the characteristic width of the model itself.

I read "significant" as "significantly different from zero." If that is the case, then you can define some simple behavior for compound model operations:

| Op | Bounds |
| - | - |
| * | intersection |
| +, - | union |
| / | LHS if RHS is unbounded, else undefined |

etc.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16951/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16951/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Description

Implemented a default bounding box for compound models. The bounding box is deduced from the compound model's operands. For example, the bounding box of the sum of two models is the union of the bounding boxes of the individual models.

Fixes #16951.

I would like to have a function like [`np.ma.clump_masked`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ma.clump_masked.html) or [`np.ma.clump_unmasked`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ma.clump_unmasked.html) that I can use on ordinary arrays to find runs of elements with identical values. My application is in astronomy, where I am computing a boolean array that represents whether a target is observable in a given time step. I want to convert this to a list of time intervals.

Under the hood, both of these functions call a helper called `_ezclump` that has the following docstring:

```
    Return list of slices corresponding to the unmasked clumps of a 1-D array.
    (A "clump" is defined as a contiguous region of the array).
```

This is almost exactly what I want. (Really, I want to clump values along each row in a 2D array, and return a list of a list of slices... but this is close enough.)

This could be called `np.flat_clump_nonzero` or something similar.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27374/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27374/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16998","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16998/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16998/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/16998/events","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/16998","id":2519937458,"node_id":"I_kwDOAB_CCc6WMzGy","number":16998,"title":"Declination column missing when writing tables with SkyCoord columns to ECSV","user":{"login":"lpsinger","id":728407,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyODQwNw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/728407?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger","html_url":"https://github.com/lpsinger","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lpsinger/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":16191481,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjE5MTQ4MQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/Bug","name":"Bug","color":"d93f0b","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-11T14:37:12Z","updated_at":"2024-09-11T14:38:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

If you create a new SkyCoord instance from an existing one, passing the `representation_type=` kwarg to the constructor, add it to a table, and write the table to ECSV, columns are missing.

For example, this code:

```python
coords = SkyCoord(np.zeros(5), np.ones(5), np.zeros(5), representation_type=CartesianRepresentation)
coords = SkyCoord(coords, representation_type=UnitSphericalRepresentation)
Table({"coords": coords}).write(sys.stdout, format="ascii.ecsv")
```

results in the following table output, missing the declination column:

```
# %ECSV 1.0
# ---
# datatype:
# - {name: coords.ra, unit: deg, datatype: float64}
# meta: !!omap
# - __serialized_columns__:
#     coords:
#       __class__: astropy.coordinates.sky_coordinate.SkyCoord
#       frame: icrs
#       ra: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn
#         __class__: astropy.coordinates.angles.core.Longitude
#         unit: &id001 !astropy.units.Unit {unit: deg}
#         value: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn {name: coords.ra}
#         wrap_angle: !astropy.coordinates.Angle
#           unit: *id001
#           value: 360.0
#       representation_type: unitspherical
# schema: astropy-2.0
coords.ra
90.0
90.0
90.0
90.0
90.0
```

### Expected behavior

Each of the three examples in the code below should print:

```
# %ECSV 1.0
# ---
# datatype:
# - {name: coords.ra, unit: deg, datatype: float64}
# - {name: coords.dec, unit: deg, datatype: float64}
# meta: !!omap
# - __serialized_columns__:
#     coords:
#       __class__: astropy.coordinates.sky_coordinate.SkyCoord
#       dec: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn
#         __class__: astropy.coordinates.angles.core.Latitude
#         unit: &id001 !astropy.units.Unit {unit: deg}
#         value: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn {name: coords.dec}
#       frame: icrs
#       ra: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn
#         __class__: astropy.coordinates.angles.core.Longitude
#         unit: *id001
#         value: !astropy.table.SerializedColumn {name: coords.ra}
#         wrap_angle: !astropy.coordinates.Angle
#           unit: *id001
#           value: 360.0
#       representation_type: spherical
# schema: astropy-2.0
coords.ra coords.dec
90.0 0.0
90.0 0.0
90.0 0.0
90.0 0.0
90.0 0.0
```

However, only the first two tables print that. The last table is missing the declination column.

### How to Reproduce

```python
from astropy import units as u
from astropy.coordinates import CartesianRepresentation, UnitSphericalRepresentation, SkyCoord
import numpy as np
from astropy.table import Table
import sys

coords = SkyCoord(np.full(5, 90), np.zeros(5), unit=u.deg)
Table({"coords": coords}).write(sys.stdout, format="ascii.ecsv")

coords = SkyCoord(np.zeros(5), np.ones(5), np.zeros(5), representation_type=CartesianRepresentation)
coords.representation_type = UnitSphericalRepresentation
Table({"coords": coords}).write(sys.stdout, format="ascii.ecsv")

coords = SkyCoord(np.zeros(5), np.ones(5), np.zeros(5), representation_type=CartesianRepresentation)
coords = SkyCoord(coords, representation_type=UnitSphericalRepresentation)
Table({"coords": coords}).write(sys.stdout, format="ascii.ecsv")
```

### Versions

```
Python 3.11.10 (main, Sep  7 2024, 05:47:53) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
astropy 6.1.3
Numpy 2.1.1
pyerfa 2.0.1.4
Scipy 1.14.1
Matplotlib 3.9.2
```

pull request is to address a bug in how coordinates in tables are written out: if the data are unitspherical, the last representation name was removed, which only is OK for the (default) case in which the representation class is spherical. The corrects that, as well as similar problems for proper motion and radial-velocity.

In the process, also allows things like `ICRS(ra, dec, radial_velocity=..., differential_type="radial")` (which raised on `radial_velocity` before, since the default name was not set).

p.s. All this is a lot of hassle just because the frames insist on hiding the actual data inside different representations -- and then wish to write those for-show ones to files instead of using the true underlying data. But that's for another time...

pull request is to address a bug in how coordinates in tables are written out: if the data are unitspherical, the last representation name was removed, which only is OK for the (default) case in which the representation class is spherical. The corrects that, as well as similar problems for proper motion and radial-velocity.

In the process, also allows things like `ICRS(ra, dec, radial_velocity=..., differential_type="radial")` (which raised on `radial_velocity` before, since the default name was not set).

p.s. All this is a lot of hassle just because the frames insist on hiding the actual data inside different representations -- and then wish to write those for-show ones to files instead of using the true underlying data. But that's for another time...

Request","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-12T02:14:33Z","updated_at":"2024-09-12T02:14:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Currently, [Numpy array creation functions cannot be used to initialize Quantity](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/known_issues.html#id4). However, @maxnoe speculates:

> I think it could be implemented now using the like option. Passing an empty quantity to like now creates an error inside astropy:
> 
> ```
> In [12]: np.arange(0 * u.m, 0.1 * u.m, 0.01 * u.m, like=u.Quantity([], u.m))
> WARNING: function 'arange' is not known to astropy's Quantity. Will run it anyway, hoping it will treat ndarray subclasses correctly. Please raise an issue at https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues. [astropy.units.quantity]
> ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
> AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
> Cell In[12], line 1
> ----> 1 np.arange(0 * u.m, 0.1 * u.m, 0.01 * u.m, like=u.Quantity([], u.m))
> 
> File ~/.local/conda/envs/cta-dev/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/units/quantity.py:1888, in Quantity.__array_function__(self, function, types, args, kwargs)
>    1880 else:
>    1881     warnings.warn(
>    1882         f"function '{function.__name__}' is not known to astropy's Quantity."
>    1883         " Will run it anyway, hoping it will treat ndarray subclasses"
>    (...)
>    1886         AstropyWarning,
>    1887     )
> -> 1888     return super().__array_function__(function, types, args, kwargs)
>    1890 # If unit is None, a plain array is expected (e.g., boolean), which
>    1891 # means we're done.
>    1892 # We're also done if the result was NotImplemented, which can happen
>    1893 # if other inputs/outputs override __array_function__;
>    1894 # hopefully, they can then deal with us.
>    1895 if unit is None or result is NotImplemented:
> 
> AttributeError: 'builtin_function_or_method' object has no attribute '_implementation'
> ```

### Describe the desired outcome

Enable the Numpy array creation routines for use with quantities: [full](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.full.html#numpy.full), [zeros](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.zeros.html#numpy.zeros), [ones](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ones.html#numpy.ones), [empty](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.empty.html#numpy.empty), and [arange](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.arange.html#numpy.arange).

### Additional context

What is the problem this feature will solve?

Currently, [Numpy array creation functions cannot be used to initialize Quantity](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/known_issues.html#id4). However, @maxnoe speculates:

> I think it could be implemented now using the like option. Passing an empty quantity to like now creates an error inside astropy:
> 
> ```
> In [12]: np.arange(0 * u.m, 0.1 * u.m, 0.01 * u.m, like=u.Quantity([], u.m))
> WARNING: function 'arange' is not known to astropy's Quantity. Will run it anyway, hoping it will treat ndarray subclasses correctly. Please raise an issue at https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues. [astropy.units.quantity]
> ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
> AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
> Cell In[12], line 1
> ----> 1 np.arange(0 * u.m, 0.1 * u.m, 0.01 * u.m, like=u.Quantity([], u.m))
> 
> File ~/.local/conda/envs/cta-dev/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/units/quantity.py:1888, in Quantity.__array_function__(self, function, types, args, kwargs)
>    1880 else:
>    1881     warnings.warn(
>    1882         f"function '{function.__name__}' is not known to astropy's Quantity."
>    1883         " Will run it anyway, hoping it will treat ndarray subclasses"
>    (...)
>    1886         AstropyWarning,
>    1887     )
> -> 1888     return super().__array_function__(function, types, args, kwargs)
>    1890 # If unit is None, a plain array is expected (e.g., boolean), which
>    1891 # means we're done.
>    1892 # We're also done if the result was NotImplemented, which can happen
>    1893 # if other inputs/outputs override __array_function__;
>    1894 # hopefully, they can then deal with us.
>    1895 if unit is None or result is NotImplemented:
> 
> AttributeError: 'builtin_function_or_method' object has no attribute '_implementation'
> ```

### Describe the desired outcome

Enable the Numpy array creation routines for use with quantities: [full](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.full.html#numpy.full), [zeros](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.zeros.html#numpy.zeros), [ones](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ones.html#numpy.ones), [empty](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.empty.html#numpy.empty), and [arange](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.arange.html#numpy.arange).

Description

When you try to concatenate to two `Table` instances that have `SkyCoord` columns, the concatenation fails if the SkyCoord instances in either table have ever undergone a change of representation.

### Expected behavior

The script below should finish without raising any exceptions.

### How to Reproduce

```python
from astropy.table import Table, vstack
from astropy.coordinates import CartesianRepresentation, SkyCoord, UnitSphericalRepresentation
from astropy import units as u

# This is fine
coords = SkyCoord([0] * u.deg, [90] * u.deg, representation_type=UnitSphericalRepresentation)
t1 = Table({"a": coords})
coords = SkyCoord([0] * u.deg, [0] * u.deg, representation_type=UnitSphericalRepresentation)
t2 = Table({"a": coords})
vstack((t1, t2))

# This raises an exception
coords = SkyCoord([0], [0], [1], unit=u.m, representation_type=CartesianRepresentation)
coords.representation_type = UnitSphericalRepresentation
t1 = Table({"a": coords})
coords = SkyCoord([0] * u.deg, [0] * u.deg, representation_type=UnitSphericalRepresentation)
t2 = Table({"a": coords})
vstack((t1, t2))
```

This script raises the following error:

```pytb
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/coordinates/baseframe.py", line 282, in new_like
    out[0] = coord[0]
    ~~~^^^
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/coordinates/sky_coordinate.py", line 388, in __setitem__
    self._sky_coord_frame[item] = value._sky_coord_frame
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/coordinates/baseframe.py", line 1787, in __setitem__
    raise TypeError(
TypeError: can only set from object of same class: CartesianRepresentation vs. UnitSphericalRepresentation

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/test.py", line 18, in <module>
    vstack((t1, t2))
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/table/operations.py", line 718, in vstack
    out = _vstack(tables, join_type, metadata_conflicts)
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/table/operations.py", line 1494, in _vstack
    col = col_cls.info.new_like(cols, n_rows, metadata_conflicts, out_name)
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/Users/lpsinger/src/m4opt/.vscode/env/lib/python3.11/site-packages/astropy/coordinates/baseframe.py", line 284, in new_like

ValueError: Input coords are inconsistent.

What is the problem this feature will solve?

I need to find duplicate entries in a SkyCoord object. These are not near matches (#14486). These are actual duplicates. These are repeated visits to reference fields for a survey telescope in an observing plan. I'd like to be able to use `np.unique` on SkyCoord objects. `np.unique` currently fails with with the exception `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'SkyCoord' and 'SkyCoord'`.

### Describe the desired outcome

`np.unique` works on SkyCoord objects.

### Additional context

Request","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-13T19:57:51Z","updated_at":"2024-08-13T19:57:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Please make it possible to store Frame objects that do not have data in Astropy tables.

I am working on an Astropy-based observation planning and scheduling toolkit for space missions, primarily for tiled imaging observations with wide-field cameras. A natural way for my code to represent the fields to observe is as SkyOffsetICRS objects without any attached data: these objects represent the RA, Dec, and roll of the spacecraft for the observation.

I would like my code to be able to save the planned timeline of observations as a Table which has a column of type SkyOffsetICRS. However, I find that Astropy does not allow me to do this. Here's a minimal reproducer:

```python
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord, SkyOffsetFrame
from astropy.table import Table
from astropy import units as u
import numpy as np

ra = [1, 2, 3] * u.deg
de = [4, 5, 6] * u.deg
pa = [7, 8, 9] * u.deg

# SkyOffsetFrame with dummy values is happy, but it's not what I want.
dummy = np.zeros(3) * u.deg
frame_with_dummy_data = SkyOffsetFrame(
    dummy, dummy, origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa)
Table({'frame': frame_with_dummy_data})

# SkyCoord with dummy values is happy, but it's not what I want either.
dummy_coord_with_frame = SkyCoord(
    dummy, dummy, frame=SkyOffsetFrame(origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa))
Table({'coord': dummy_coord_with_frame})

# This is what I want: a bare frame with no data. But the Table constructor
# raises the following exception: `ValueError: The frame object
# "<SkyOffsetICRS ...>" does not have associated data`
frame = SkyOffsetFrame(origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa)
Table({'frame': frame})
```

### Describe the desired outcome

Add support for adding Frame objects that do not have data as columns in tables.


### Additional context

frame = SkyOffsetFrame(origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa)
Table({'frame': frame})
```

### Describe the desired outcome

Add support for adding Frame objects that do not have data as columns in tables.

Request","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":null},{"id":229192,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMjkxOTI=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/table","name":"table","color":"c7def8","default":false,"description":null},{"id":21499868,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMTQ5OTg2OA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/labels/coordinates","name":"coordinates","color":"c7def8","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-08-13T19:57:51Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T18:35:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Please make it possible to store Frame objects that do not have data in Astropy tables.

I am working on an Astropy-based observation planning and scheduling toolkit for space missions, primarily for tiled imaging observations with wide-field cameras. A natural way for my code to represent the fields to observe is as SkyOffsetICRS objects without any attached data: these objects represent the RA, Dec, and roll of the spacecraft for the observation.

I would like my code to be able to save the planned timeline of observations as a Table which has a column of type SkyOffsetICRS. However, I find that Astropy does not allow me to do this. Here's a minimal reproducer:

```python
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord, SkyOffsetFrame
from astropy.table import Table
from astropy import units as u
import numpy as np

ra = [1, 2, 3] * u.deg
de = [4, 5, 6] * u.deg
pa = [7, 8, 9] * u.deg

# SkyOffsetFrame with dummy values is happy, but it's not what I want.
dummy = np.zeros(3) * u.deg
frame_with_dummy_data = SkyOffsetFrame(
    dummy, dummy, origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa)
Table({'frame': frame_with_dummy_data})

# SkyCoord with dummy values is happy, but it's not what I want either.
dummy_coord_with_frame = SkyCoord(
    dummy, dummy, frame=SkyOffsetFrame(origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa))
Table({'coord': dummy_coord_with_frame})

# This is what I want: a bare frame with no data. But the Table constructor
# raises the following exception: `ValueError: The frame object
# "<SkyOffsetICRS ...>" does not have associated data`
frame = SkyOffsetFrame(origin=SkyCoord(ra, de), rotation=pa)
Table({'frame': frame})
```

### Describe the desired outcome

Add support for adding Frame objects that do not have data as columns in tables.


### Additional context

pull request is to address the fact that coordinate frames cannot be written to tables (with or without data). It does this in two commits:
1. Let BaseCoordinateFrame have an `.info` attribute. Here, since the code is almost the same as that for SkyCoord, I base `SkyCoordInfo` on the new `CoordinateFrameInfo`.  Also, to avoid duplication of `.insert` methods, I define an `_insert` method on the info class, which can be used for both `BaseCoordinateFrame` and `SkyCoord`.
2. Allow the use of `BaseCoordinateFrame` in tables, including tests for `SkyOffsetFrame` with and without associated data. 

To get this to work, `Table` can no longer rely on the input having a `len` - since data-less coordinate frames raise on that. So, instead it prefers `col.shape[0]` if that is available. Obviously, instead one could give data-less frames a `len`, but that seemed a rather big API change (even though it is perhaps reasonable). Furthermore, since `SkyOffsetFrame` classes are defined on the fly, I treat these as special cases in the white list of allowed classes for serialization.

Description

Models that have input units cannot be used for fitting. See traceback below.

This is strange because the fitter is smart enough to temporarily strip off the units from the parameters.

The reproducer below is a bit contrived, but the real application here is fitting dimensionful dust extinction models (see https://github.com/karllark/dust_extinction/issues/237).

### Expected behavior

Fitting should succeed. The fitter should create a new temporary model that has the input units stripped out, run the fitting on that, and then restore the units and return the original model.

### How to Reproduce

### Dimensionless: works fine

This code, in which the input data is dimensionless, works fine:

```python
from astropy.modeling import Fittable1DModel, Parameter
from astropy.modeling.fitting import LevMarLSQFitter
from astropy import units as u
import numpy as np


class DimensionlessFoobar1D(Fittable1DModel):

    n_inputs = 1
    n_outputs = 1
    input_units = {"x": u.dimensionless_unscaled}
    return_units = {"y": u.dimensionless_unscaled}
    _input_units_strict = True

    a = Parameter(default=1.0)

    def _parameter_units_for_data_units(self, inputs_unit, outputs_unit):
        # Declare that all of the parameters are dimensionless,
        # regardless of the input units.
        return dict.fromkeys(self.param_names)

    @staticmethod
    def evaluate(x, a):
        return np.tanh(x * a)

    def fit_deriv(self, x, a):
        return [x * (1 - np.square(self.evaluate(x, a)))]


xdata = np.linspace(-10, 10)
ydata = DimensionlessFoobar1D(a=3.14)(xdata) + 0.01 * np.random.randn(len(xdata))

model_init = DimensionlessFoobar1D()
fitter = LevMarLSQFitter()
fitter(model_init, xdata, ydata)
```

The output is:

```
<DimensionlessFoobar1D(a=3.17953878)>
```

### Dimensionful: fails

This version fails:

```python
from astropy.modeling import Fittable1DModel, Parameter
from astropy.modeling.fitting import LevMarLSQFitter
from astropy import units as u
import numpy as np


class DimensionfulFoobar1D(Fittable1DModel):

    n_inputs = 1
    n_outputs = 1
    input_units = {"x": u.yottaHenry}
    return_units = {"y": u.dimensionless_unscaled}
    _input_units_strict = True

    a = Parameter(default=1.0)

    def _parameter_units_for_data_units(self, inputs_unit, outputs_unit):
        # Declare that all of the parameters are dimensionless,
        # regardless of the input units.
        return dict.fromkeys(self.param_names)

    @staticmethod
    def evaluate(x, a):
        return np.tanh((x / u.yottaHenry).to_value(u.dimensionless_unscaled) * a)

    def fit_deriv(self, x, a):
        return [x / u.yottaHenry * (1 - np.square(self.evaluate(x, a)))]


xdata = np.linspace(-10, 10) * u.yottaHenry
ydata = DimensionfulFoobar1D(a=3.14)(xdata) + 0.01 * np.random.randn(len(xdata))

model_init = DimensionfulFoobar1D()
fitter = LevMarLSQFitter()
fitter(model_init, xdata, ydata)
```

I agree. Proposed fix in #220.

> Bug - there is no reason this code should be issuing a scary sounding warning for a situation that is expected and normal. This warning ended up making me not trust the results and I spent an hour tracking down what was happening and submitting this issue.

It would be easy enough to remove the warning; we would just remove this line: https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/v1.0.3/astropy_healpix/_core.c#L302.

However, that would make this method inconsistent with the others; all of the functions set the invalid status if they return any invalid pixel values.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy-healpix/issues/comments/2235154875/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

Hi all,

When I install healpy using conda-forge on an M1 mac it includes its own copy of libomp in (for e.g. py3.12) ${CONDA_PREFIX}/lib/python3.12/site-packages/healpy/.dylibs/libomp.dylib. When I use healpy at the same time as, for example, scipy, which links to a shared conda version in ${CONDA_PREFIX}/lib/libomp.dylib, this causes a crash at runtime with this message:

OMP: Error #15: Initializing libomp.dylib, but found libomp.dylib already initialized.
OMP: Hint This means that multiple copies of the OpenMP runtime have been linked into the program. That is dangerous, since it can degrade performance or cause incorrect results. The best thing to do is to ensure that only a single OpenMP runtime is linked into the process, e.g. by avoiding static linking of the OpenMP runtime in any library. As an unsafe, unsupported, undocumented workaround you can set the environment variable KMP_DUPLICATE_LIB_OK=TRUE to allow the program to continue to execute, but that may cause crashes or silently produce incorrect results. For more information, please see http://openmp.llvm.org/
The workaround it suggests works for me I don't yet know if any of the problems it warns about actually occur.


Cheers,
Joe

Upon careful reading of the `PyArray_RegisterDataType` function, I think that it has different reference counting behavior for Numpy 1.x vs. 2.x. In Numpy 1.x, on success, a reference is stolen because the argument is added to the list of user descrs. However, in Numpy 2.x, the reference is never stolen.

So if one creates a `PyArray_DescrProto` on the heap and passes it to `PyArray_RegisterDataType`, one must remember to `Py_DECREF` it on success on Python 2.x but one must _not_ on Python 1.x.

Normally to allocate it on the heap you would do the following:

```c
PyArray_DescrProto *proto = PyObject_New(PyArray_DescrProto, &PyArrayDescr_Type);
```

However if you ever call `Py_DECREF` on it, Python will crash in the `tp_dealloc` function for `PyArrayDescr_Type` because of the differences in struct layout between `PyArray_DescrProto` and `PyArray_Descr`.

I think it is best to always treat `PyArray_DescrProto` objects as immortal and to give them static storage duration. I have an unfortunate application where I really must allocate them dynamically, but I think that I can make that work by using `PyObject_Malloc` and `PyObject_Free`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2181177478/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
In file included from /opt/python/cp312-cp312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/_core/include/numpy/ndarrayobject.h:28,
                 from /opt/python/cp312-cp312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/_core/include/numpy/arrayobject.h:5,
                 from cs_gamma.c:40:
/opt/python/cp312-cp312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/_core/include/numpy/npy_2_compat.h:77:1: error: function declaration isn’t a prototype [-Werror=strict-prototypes]
   77 | PyArray_ImportNumPyAPI()
      | ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

In order to suppress this error, we change the declaration from 

```c
static inline int
PyArray_ImportNumPyAPI()
```

to

```c
static inline int
PyArray_ImportNumPyAPI(void)
```

Upon careful reading of the `PyArray_RegisterDataType` function, I think that it has different reference counting behavior for Numpy 1.x vs. 2.x. In Numpy 1.x, on success, a reference is stolen because the argument is added to the list of user descrs. However, in Numpy 2.x, the reference is never stolen.

So if one creates a `PyArray_DescrProto` on the heap and passes it to `PyArray_RegisterDataType`, one must remember to `Py_DECREF` it on success on Python 2.x but one must _not_ on Python 1.x.

```python

```

Describe your issue.

`scipy.spatial.Delaunay` and `scipy.interpolate.LinearNDInterpolator` segfault on inputs of invalid shapes.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from scipy.spatial import Delaunay
>>> Delaunay(np.random.randn(4, 10, 3))


or

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from scipy.interpolate import LinearNDInterpolator
>>> LinearNDInterpolator(np.random.randn(4, 10, 3), np.random.randn(10))
```
```


### Error message

```shell
Segmentation fault: 11
```
```

Sphinx docs builds for third-party packages that have intersphinx cross-references to matplotlib are now failing with the following error message:

```
Unknown interpreted text role "mpltype".
```

This is because #27557 and Matplotlib 3.9.0 added the `:mpltype:` custom role to the docs. Would you please provide some guidance for how packages that depend on Matplotlib should be building their sphinx docs?

PR has been triggered in an effort to update **numpy2**. Here are some more details about this specific migrator: > > TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot > PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details). > The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release > channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main > `conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA. > > The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy > version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy > will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions. > > Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a > run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a > run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove > any occurrences of this. > > However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package > is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should > check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you > need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy > issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages). > > Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the > final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages > that can be published to our main channels. > > If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do > ``` > conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc > ``` > or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly. > > ### To-Dos: > * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility) > * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`. > * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases. > * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`. > * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed. > > PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that > the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until > a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this > feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in > case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).

Here are some more details about this specific migrator: > > TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot > PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details). > The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release > channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main > `conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA. > > The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy > version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy > will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions. > > Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a > run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a > run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove > any occurrences of this. > > However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package > is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should > check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you > need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy > issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages). > > Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the > final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages > that can be published to our main channels. > > If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do > ``` > conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc > ``` > or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly. > > ### To-Dos: > * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility) > * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`. > * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases. > * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`. > * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed. > > PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that > the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until > a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this > feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in > case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).

{"repository_id":60295236,"push_id":18457080887,"size":2,"distinct_size":2,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"e21e521bd4ceaea9747a858de6900b8817db028c","before":"22606f7406e60e62a14e431a6d173da6d819ac15","commits":[{"sha":"532ff9380187cbf4c50c208ee6dce4ca2f8db051","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).
The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release
channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main
`conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA.

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the
final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages
that can be published to our main channels.

If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do
```
conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc
```
or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly.

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in
case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/healpy-feedstock/commits/532ff9380187cbf4c50c208ee6dce4ca2f8db051"},{"sha":"e21e521bd4ceaea9747a858de6900b8817db028c","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"MNT: Re-rendered with conda-build 24.5.0, conda-smithy 3.35.1, and conda-forge-pinning 2024.05.16.16.45.08","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/healpy-feedstock/commits/e21e521bd4ceaea9747a858de6900b8817db028c"}]}

<!--
Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

Checklist
- [ ] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [ ] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [ ] Source is from official source.
- [ ] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [ ] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [ ] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [ ] Build number is 0.

- [ ] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [ ] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).

<!--
Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

Checklist
- [ ] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [ ] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [ ] Source is from official source.
- [ ] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [ ] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [ ] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [ ] Build number is 0.
- [ ] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/issues/247","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/issues/247/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/issues/247/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/issues/247/events","html_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/issues/247","id":2221078680,"node_id":"I_kwDOBjbJgM6EYviY","number":247,"title":"Trailing backticks in markdown files","user":{"login":"oscarbenjamin","id":1159732,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNTk3MzI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1159732?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin","html_url":"https://github.com/oscarbenjamin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":698231415,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2OTgyMzE0MTU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"ee0701","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-04-02T17:40:50Z","updated_at":"2024-04-03T14:22:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This follows gh-220. After gh-221 it is documented that a markdown block needs to end with a blank line before the backticks like:
``````markdown
```
>>> 1 + 1
2

```
``````
I think that this should be considered a bug so I'm opening this issue.

The source of the bug is the stdlib doctest module (https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/116546). It probably does not make sense to fix it at the stdlib level because the stdlib doctest parser is very rudimentary and does not even know that it is parsing markdown syntax at all. There is also a pytest issue (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/7374) which was closed because pytest's doctest support is unmaintained.

It seems to me that here in pytest-doctestplus is the place to fix this since markdown files for documentation are common.

The erroneous result from the stdlib parser comes in here at the start of parsing:
https://github.com/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/blob/84fd2e2c37dbce4708b8045692fc56c016262332/pytest_doctestplus/plugin.py#L393-L394

To handle markdown properly the parser would need to be designed for markdown. I suggest having an alternate `MarkdownParser` class that would look for backticks rather than depending on the stdlib's PS1 regex. The rst directives would ideally also have markdown variants but I am not sure how those could look. I have been relying upon the `add_newdoc_ufunc` function to add docstrings to those functions because it is such a pain to write legible multiline strings in C. According to the [Numpy 2.0.0 migration guide](https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#main-namespace), `add_newdoc_ufunc` is \"an internal function and doesn’t have a replacement.\" Is it possible to add it back to the public namespace as `numpy.lib.add_newdoc_ufunc`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26233/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26233/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I have been relying upon the `add_newdoc_ufunc` function to add docstrings to those functions because it is such a pain to write legible multiline strings in C. According to the [Numpy 2.0.0 migration guide](https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#main-namespace), `add_newdoc_ufunc` is \"an internal function and doesn’t have a replacement.\" files","user":{"login":"oscarbenjamin","id":1159732,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNTk3MzI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1159732?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin","html_url":"https://github.com/oscarbenjamin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":698231415,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2OTgyMzE0MTU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"ee0701","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-04-02T17:40:50Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T14:09:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This follows gh-220. After gh-221 it is documented that a markdown block needs to end with a blank line before the backticks like:
``````markdown
```
>>> 1 + 1
2

```
``````
I think that this should be considered a bug so I'm opening this issue.

The source of the bug is the stdlib doctest module (https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/116546). It probably does not make sense to fix it at the stdlib level because the stdlib doctest parser is very rudimentary and does not even know that it is parsing markdown syntax at all. There is also a pytest issue (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/7374) which was closed because pytest's doctest support is unmaintained.

It seems to me that here in pytest-doctestplus is the place to fix this since markdown files for documentation are common.

The erroneous result from the stdlib parser comes in here at the start of parsing:
https://github.com/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/blob/84fd2e2c37dbce4708b8045692fc56c016262332/pytest_doctestplus/plugin.py#L393-L394

To handle markdown properly the parser would need to be designed for markdown. I suggest having an alternate `MarkdownParser` class that would look for backticks rather than depending on the stdlib's PS1 regex. The rst directives would ideally also have markdown variants but I am not sure how those could look. Once the fenced sections are extracted from the markdown file they can be parsed by the existing parser to separate Proposed new feature or change:

I maintain several Python packages with C extensions that define ufuncs and generalized ufuncs. I have been relying upon the `add_newdoc_ufunc` function to add docstrings to those functions because it is such a pain to write legible multiline strings in C. According to the [Numpy 2.0.0 migration guide](https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#main-namespace), `add_newdoc_ufunc` is "an internal function and doesn’t have a replacement."
